title: Research Projects
text:
Master’s Thesis — The Nature of Socio-Emotional Competencies (Ongoing)


Title: Zur Natur sozioemotionaler Kompetenzen: Eine Konstruktvalidierung der Trennbarkeit von Skills und Traits über Struktur, Stabilität und Messartefakte.


Core Question: Do socio-emotional skills represent a distinct, malleable construct (“I can”), or are they merely a “re-labeling” of stable personality traits (“I am”)?


Approach: Using a construct-validation approach, I investigate the empirical distinctness of skills (measured via BESSI-G) and traits (Big Five). The project addresses the “trait-re-labeling” debate by analyzing structural overlap and differential stability.


Methodological Highlights:



Exploratory Structural Equation Modeling (ESEM): To account for the complex factor structure of social-behavioral skills.


Longitudinal Modeling: Assessing rank-order stability over a school year using latent autoregressive models (TOSCA-NEO sample).


Measurement Invariance: Testing the stability of the measurement model across time.


Tools: R (lavaan), Mplus, Quarto for reproducible reporting, FIML for attrition handling.





Research Project — Metacognitive Indicators in Mathematics Classrooms


Topic: Predicting high-inference teaching quality ratings through linguistic indicators of metacognition.


Content: Using a metacognitive framework for mathematical learning (e.g., Zepeda), we investigated how specific linguistic markers in classroom transcripts relate to expert observer ratings of teaching quality. The project explores whether automated linguistic analysis can reflect the “invisible” metacognitive modeling provided by teachers.


Key Contribution: Bridging the gap between theory-driven metacognitive frameworks and applied classroom diagnostics. It highlights the potential of linguistic cues as objective proxies for complex instructional processes in mathematics.


Methods:


Mapping linguistic indicators to dimensions of teaching quality.


Predictive modeling of observer ratings based on transcript data.


Reflection on domain-specific (math) vs. domain-general metacognitive cues.

title: Projects
text:
Master’s Thesis (ongoing) — Conceptual & Psychometric Foundations of Socio-Emotional Skills (BESSI)


Topic: Psychometric validation of a short-form instrument to assess socio-emotional skills in adolescents.


Content: In my master’s thesis, I investigate the conceptual and psychometric foundations of socio-emotional skills measurement, focusing on the distinction between skills (“I can”) and personality traits (“I am”). The project examines whether high correlations reflect substantive overlap or near-synonymous item wording.


Methods:



Large longitudinal sample of upper secondary students


Exploratory Structural Equation Modeling (ESEM)


Longitudinal measurement invariance testing


Latent stability models


R (lavaan), FIML for missing data





Secondary Analysis — ITT & CACE effects in a digital socio-emotional intervention


Topic: Compliance-sensitive causal effects in a digital socio-emotional intervention for preschool children.


Content: Secondary analysis of a randomized controlled trial. Beyond intention-to-treat (ITT), I applied Complier Average Causal Effect (CACE) models to examine how intervention effects depend on actual usage (compliance).


Methods:



Secondary analysis of an RCT


ITT vs. CACE comparison


FIML for missing data


Sensitivity and tipping-point analyses



Key contribution: ITT effects were small/absent, while CACE estimates suggested stronger effects among high-compliance participants and highlighted threats to causal interpretation when design assumptions are weak.




Experimental Study — Epistemic emotions and information-seeking


Topic: Epistemic emotions, misinformation, and exploratory information-seeking.


Content: In an online experiment, we tested whether stylistic framings of refutation texts induce epistemic emotions (confusion vs. situational interest) and influence exploratory information-seeking behaviour and comprehension.


Methods:



Online experiment (between-subjects)


Manipulation via text design


Behavioural measure (click behaviour)


Regression models, ANOVA, robustness checks



Key contribution: No clear condition differences, but the study provides a methodological analysis of limits in emotion induction and click-based proxies.




Bachelor’s Thesis — Mediation model of mindfulness, decentering, and emotional competence


Topic: Do decentering and emotional competence mediate the relationship between mindfulness and well-being?


Content: In my bachelor’s thesis, I examined whether the relationship between mindfulness and well-being can be explained by underlying mechanisms, specifically decentering and emotional competence. Using cross-sectional survey data, I tested a mediation model to explore potential indirect pathways.


Methods:



Online survey study (N = 190)


Mediation analysis using PROCESS (Hayes)


Bootstrapping (5,000 iterations)


Path analysis (SPSS AMOS)


A priori power analysis



Key contribution: While causal interpretation was limited due to the correlational design, the project introduced a mechanistic perspective on socio-emotional competencies and highlighted the need for experimental and compliance-sensitive approaches in subsequent work.

title: Likertitems als metrisch oder ordinalskaliert betrachten?
text:
Problem:
Schätzung mit Weighted Least Squares Mean and Variance adjustedund in Sewell et al. (2024) |
Vergleich mit Maximum Likelihood (ML) Schätzung, wenn die Faktorladungen relativ gleichbleiben/ Standardfehler nicht zu sehr voneinander abweichen –> weiterrechnen mit ML für weitere Analysen.

title: About
text:
Master’s Student | Empirical Educational Research & Educational Psychology
University of Tübingen (Expected graduation: October 2026)
My focus is on the conceptualization, measurement, and causal interpretation of non-cognitive competencies in educational contexts, with a particular emphasis on socio-emotional and self-regulatory skills. I have worked with quantitative and qualitative approaches, including longitudinal measurement models, secondary analyses of randomized interventions.
My methodological focus lies in quantitative research designs and psychometric approaches.






While socio-emotional and self-regulatory skills are key to educational success, their measurement often faces a ‘validity gap.’ I am interested in closing this gap by investigating whether we are measuring stable traits or malleable skills—and how we can design interventions that lead to robust, causally interpretable improvements.



Across projects, I am interested in aligning substantive research questions with appropriate measurement strategies and analytic approaches, and in reflecting on the assumptions underlying causal interpretation in applied educational research.



I aim to pursue a PhD where I can contribute to making ‘non-cognitive’ growth measurable and actionable. My goal is to move from ‘black-box’ interventions to a precise understanding of what works for whom and why.

title: Research Projects
text:
Completed Projects


Causal Evaluation — ITT & CACE Effects in Digital Interventions


Project: Secondary analysis of a Randomized Controlled Trial (RCT) evaluating the digital empathy-training “Zirkus Empathico”.


Focus: Moving beyond the traditional Intention-to-Treat (ITT) perspective to account for varied usage (compliance) in home-based settings.



CACE-Modeling: Estimating the “Complier Average Causal Effect” to isolate the effect of actual intervention dosage.


Robustness Checks: Handling missing data via Full Information Maximum Likelihood (FIML) and conducting MNAR Tipping-Point Analyses to test the sensitivity of results against non-random attrition.



Key Insight: While ITT effects were delayed, CACE estimates revealed stronger potential effects for high-compliance users, while simultaneously highlighting the risks of causal interpretation when design assumptions are challenged.




Experimental Study — Epistemic Emotions & Information-Seeking


Project: An online experiment on the impact of linguistic framing in refutation texts.


Research Goal: Examining whether subtle emotional framing (confusion vs. interest) influences how learners engage with scientific misinformation and exploratory “click-behavior”.



Design: Between-subjects experiment (N=134) with behavioral proxies for information-seeking.


Analysis: Regression models and robustness checks for emotion induction.


Reflection: A methodological critique of “click-based” behavioral measures and the limits of retrospective emotion reporting.





Bachelor’s Thesis — Mechanisms of Well-being


Title: Verwobene Wege zum Wohlbefinden: Achtsamkeit, Dezentrierung und emotionale Kompetenz.


Research Goal: Identifying the “how” and “why” behind the benefits of mindfulness by testing a mediation model.



Methods: Path analysis and mediation modeling (SPSS AMOS & PROCESS).


Insight: This project laid the foundation for my interest in protective factors, while highlighting the need for the advanced causal and longitudinal methods I now apply in my Master’s work.

title: Research Projects
text:
Technical & Open Science Commitment
All projects are conducted with a strong commitment to Open Science principles: * Reproducibility: Analysis scripts are written in R, utilizing Quarto for fully transparent and automated reporting. * Rigorous Modeling: Preference for latent variable models (SEM) to account for measurement error. * Causal Thinking: Explicit reflection on the assumptions (e.g., Exclusion Restriction in CACE) necessary for causal claims in educational research.
